subject: Chemistry
title: The Thermite Reaction
description: A quick, practical guide to The Thermite Reaction.

hook.text: Mix iron oxide and aluminum powder, light it, and it burns at 2,500°C—hot enough to melt through steel.

buildup.text: Thermite is an exothermic reaction where aluminum steals oxygen from iron oxide at extreme speed.

discovery.text: The reaction produces molten iron and aluminum oxide. It's so hot it can weld railroad tracks on site.

twist.text: Once started, thermite can't be stopped. Water makes it worse—steam explosions scatter molten metal.

climax.text: Railroad companies still use thermite welding today. Two powders create a forge anywhere.

punchline.text: Rust plus aluminum powder. Instant forge at 2,500°C.

quiz.question: Why can't you extinguish a thermite reaction with water?
options: The extreme heat causes steam explosions that scatter molten metal
Water is a fuel for thermite reactions
Thermite absorbs water and grows larger
